title: Audio Essay
description: Give it a topic — it generates two ready-to-use prompts: one optimized for deep research, one for writing a 3,000-word audio essay from that research.
template:
I'm producing a ~3000-word audio essay to be listened to via text-to-speech.
My workflow has two steps: (1) deep research, then (2) prose synthesis.

Topic: {{topic}}

Analyze this topic and produce two ready-to-use prompts:

---

PROMPT 1 — DEEP RESEARCH
This will be used in a research-specialized model (Gemini Deep Research,
Perplexity, ChatGPT Deep Research, etc.). The goal is factual completeness and
accuracy, not prose quality. Optimize this prompt for:
- Full coverage of the topic's key subtopics
- Concrete examples, named frameworks, specific terminology
- Any domain-specific nuances that a generalist model might miss or
  get wrong
- No length constraint — depth over brevity

---

PROMPT 2 — PROSE SYNTHESIS
This will be used in a prose-focused model (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini etc.) after the
research output is in hand. The research output will be pasted in. Optimize
this prompt for the following requirements:

LENGTH & FORMAT
- Approximately 3000 words of continuous, flowing prose
- No headers, bullets, numbered lists, or section labels
- End with a short References section, then three concise areas for deeper
  learning appropriate for expert-level readers

AUDIO OPTIMIZATION
- Written to be HEARD, not read
- Varied sentence rhythm and spoken transitions between ideas
- A strong opening hook; a resonant close that is not a summary
- Address the specific audio challenges this topic presents (e.g., visual
  concepts that need tactile reframing, technical vocabulary that must land
  without footnotes)

STYLE AND LANGUAGE
- Spell out all numbers, dates, and quantities in full:
  write "nineteen fifties" not "1950s," "five percent" not "5%,"
  "first" not "1st," "twenty twenty-four" not "2024"
- Write out all symbols as words: percent, dollar, and — never %, $, &
- No abbreviations or acronyms unless the full form is written out first
- No inline citations such as "(Smith, 1999)" or "[1]" — collect all
  sources in the References section at the end

AUDIENCE AND DEPTH
- Write for a highly intelligent audience that already knows something
  about the topic; go beyond common knowledge and surface-level facts
- Incorporate historical, cultural, and technical context where it deepens
  understanding
- Use metaphors or analogies sparingly and only when they improve clarity

FACTUAL RIGOR
- Anchor claims in precise details: exact figures, dates, names, dimensions
- If a claim uses a superlative or comparative ("the first," "the deepest,"
  "transformative"), immediately follow with the specific evidence — what
  measurement, what year, what it surpassed or replaced
- Do not leave generalizations unsupported; if something was significant,
  state specifically what changed, how it was measured, and what followed

TONE
- Precise, information-dense, and void of filler words and throat-clearing
  phrases ("it is worth noting," "in many ways," "as we can see")

GROUNDING
- Use the pasted research as factual grounding; do not invent facts,
  figures, or citations

title: Figure Replacement
description: Upload any image — it replaces the subject's head and face with that of a classical Roman athlete, preserving the original pose, artistic medium, and style exactly.
template:
Edit the uploaded image to replace the character's head and face with that of a 19-year-old male Roman athlete, strictly maintaining the original pose, angle, and perspective.

Character Details (Adapted to Style):

Face: A classical facial structure featuring an aquiline nose and high cheekbones. Crucially, these features must not be rendered realistically if the source image is stylized; they must be interpreted and rendered using only the strokes, lines, and abstraction level of the original artwork.

Hair: Very short hair. The texture of the hair must be defined entirely by the artistic medium of the source image (e.g., cross-hatching or engraved lines). No headgear.

Expression: An expression that is natural and consistent with the body language and context of the original pose.

Physique & Proportions: The figure should have a natural, athletic build reminiscent of classical Greco-Roman sculpture. Focus on sculptural muscle mass and functional strength rather than extreme bodybuilding proportions. The musculature should be well-defined but avoid hyper-vascularity or exaggerated "cartoonish" volume. Ensure the traps and shoulders are proportionate to a natural athlete, maintaining a sleek, alluring, and realistic silhouette.

Style & Technical Execution:

Seamless Integration: The new head and refined physique must perfectly match the exact medium, substrate texture (e.g., aged paper grain), line quality, and level of detail of the source image.

Lighting & Values: Strictly adhere to the shading values, contrast, and lighting direction of the original image to ensure the swap appears authentic.

Contingency: If the original pose hides portions of the face, apply the classical aesthetic and hair type to the visible portions of the head and neck.

title: Writing Assistant
description: Paste your text — it restructures sentences and paragraphs for clarity and flow while preserving your voice.

variables.name: text
variables.label: Text to Review
variables.placeholder: Paste your text here

variations.name: Restructure & Refine
variations.description: Paste your draft. The editor reorganizes ideas across paragraphs, reorders sentences, and rewrites for flow — moving content wherever it reads stronger. Your voice and level of concision stay intact.
variations.template:
Text:
{{text}}

Instructions

Act as a senior editor revising my writing for clarity, flow, and structure. Start from the text above.

Your primary objective is to optimize the organization of ideas, even if that requires:
• Moving sentences or clauses across paragraphs
• Reordering sentences within a paragraph
• Merging or splitting sentences
• Rewriting a sentence so it fits better in a different structural position

Do not preserve the original order unless it is already optimal. If a sentence in a later paragraph strengthens an earlier idea, relocate and revise it accordingly.

Preserve my original tone, style, and level of concision. Write as if I had written the improved version myself.

Editorial standards
• Be concise, direct, and confident
• Favor economical constructions over verbosity
• Maintain rigorous American English usage (Chicago Manual of Style)
• Avoid litotes and other negative constructions
• Avoid decorative or ornamental adjectives
• Limit the use of em dashes
• Reduce repetitive use of “I” by restructuring sentences rather than deleting content

Structural heuristics
• Each paragraph should advance a single, clear idea
• Sentences should appear in the order that maximizes logical buildup and emphasis
• If clarity improves by repositioning or rewriting a sentence, do so without comment

If possible, present the revised text in canvas mode so I can edit it directly as we iterate.

title: Shipping Quote Request
description: Paste an auction listing URL — it extracts the item's physical specs and drafts a professional packing and shipping quote request, ready to send.
template:
Listing URL: {{listing_url}}
‍
Instructions

Draft an email requesting a packing and shipping quote from a shipping vendor.

Extract the physical specifications of the item from the listing URL. Focus only on details relevant to packing, handling, and transport.

Ignore: provenance, artist identity, attribution, historical context, exhibition history, or aesthetic description.

Item Category: Classify strictly by physical form (examples: Framed Painting, Sculpture, Furniture, Pedestal, Mirror, Crate, Ceramic Vessel).

Measurement rule:
Use the largest overall dimensions, including frame, base, mount, or any protruding elements.

Exclude: sight size, plate size, stretcher size, canvas size without frame, or internal component dimensions.

Condition rule:
Only include physical vulnerabilities affecting packing, such as:

Glass present

Fragile or friable surface

Heavy or oversized weight

Protruding elements

Gilded surface with losses or flaking

Structural instability

If no relevant vulnerabilities are stated, omit the condition line entirely.

Pickup logistics:
Extract the auction house or gallery name, city, state, and pickup hours if available.

Failure handling:
If you cannot access or extract item details from the listing URL, respond with:
“Unable to extract item details from the provided listing URL. Please provide the item category, dimensions, condition, and pickup location.”

Output Formatting Requirements (MANDATORY)

Output must be plain email text, no commentary before or after.

Each address line must appear on its own separate line.


Email Template

**Subject:**
Request for Shipping Quote – [Item Category] ([Pickup Location Name], [Pickup City, State])

Hello,

Could you please assist me with a quote to pack and ship the item below from [Pickup Location Name] to my home in Marblehead, Massachusetts?

**Item listing:**  
[Item Listing URL]

**Item category:**  
[Item Category]

**Dimensions:**  
[Dimensions, including units]

**Condition:**  
[Physical packing vulnerabilities only — omit entire section if none]

**Pickup location:**  
[Pickup Location Name]  
[Pickup City, State]  
(Item must be collected during the location’s designated pickup hours: [Pickup Hours].)

**Delivery address:**
Matt Weaver-Yuwono
20 Mechanic Square, Unit 5
Marblehead, MA 01945

Please provide a quote for packing and ground shipping. I am happy to provide additional information if needed.

Thank you,

Instructions

I want to test my expertise on the topic above. Create a highly challenging, expert-level quiz that covers historical context, technical details, key figures or developments, advanced concepts, distinguishing characteristics, and real-world applications or implications.

The quiz should be structured into sections, with each section containing thought-provoking questions designed to assess deep knowledge and nuanced understanding. Include a bonus section for advanced connoisseurship or specialized expertise. The difficulty level should be equivalent to an exam for subject-matter experts or professionals in the field.

At the end of the quiz, ask if I would like the answers. If I choose to receive them, provide detailed explanations in a format optimized for audio playback, using a storytelling style similar to historian Alan Taylor. Responses should be deeply researched, engaging, and expansive—offering historical context, connections between events, and rich detail rather than just isolated facts. Transitions should be smooth, descriptions vivid, and key insights framed within a broader narrative. Avoid dry lists or overly academic phrasing; instead, present explanations with the depth and flow of an engaging historical audiobook. Where applicable, naturally reference supporting sources or data points to enhance the explanation.

title: Antiques Expert Analysis
description: Upload a photo of any antique — it identifies the piece, assesses condition and authenticity, and flags signs of restoration or forgery.

variations.name: Comprehensive Analysis
variations.description: Upload a photo. You get a full expert assessment: what the piece is, when it was made, how it was constructed, and what's been repaired or altered — with a quiz available on request. Choose this for general evaluation of any antique.
variations.template:
You are an antiques expert providing information about antique furniture and objects.

When I upload a photo of an item, please provide the following:

Identification & Dating: Use visual clues and systematic analysis to identify and date the item. Specifically mention markings, labels, or unique characteristics that help in determining its age and origin. Indicate if the item appears to be an original antique or a reproduction.
History & Context: Provide a brief history of the item's category, including its typical use and historical context. Mention broader trends from the item's era that influenced its design and purpose.
Craftsmanship & Condition:
Analyze the craftsmanship (e.g., joinery techniques, materials, and artistic style) and condition of the item.
Pay particular attention to details that indicate refinishing, forgery, or restoration. Highlight any inconsistencies in wear, suspicious hardware, or other alterations that might impact the item's authenticity.
Compare the item to recognized standards or styles to assess its authenticity.
Always apply these three authentication tests to surface modifications or inconsistencies:
• Structural Continuity: Trace every primary structural member (posts, stiles, rails, legs) and check whether it flows continuously or is interrupted by unexpected seams, shims, or misaligned grain. Any transition point between components that should be continuous is a potential site of later addition or replacement—treat it as suspect until the construction method accounts for it.
• The Patina Test: Compare aging signatures (oxidation, accumulated grime, UV darkening, wear patterns) across all surfaces, especially between primary and secondary elements. A piece that has aged as a unit will show consistent patina gradients. If any component reads noticeably lighter, cleaner, or differently aged than its neighbors, flag it as a possible later replacement or a married element from a different piece.
• Stylistic Coherence: Evaluate whether every element—structural, decorative, and hardware—speaks the same period vocabulary. Authentic pieces are internally consistent. A Rococo escutcheon on a Neoclassical case, scrolled feet on an otherwise rectilinear form, or ornate carving on a utilitarian country piece all suggest later "improvements" meant to elevate perceived age or style. Ask whether each element would have left the same workshop at the same time.
Research Resources: Recommend reliable sources for further research, such as books, museums, or organizations specializing in the item's type.
Focus Areas: Always scrutinize the item for potential refinishing, forgery indicators, or condition issues. Mention any signs of restoration and whether they positively or negatively impact the piece's historical value.

Finally, ask if I would like you to create a highly challenging, expert-level quiz to test about this item that covers historical context, production techniques, design characteristics, notable makers, market factors, and authentication methods. If yes, provide a quiz to test my expertise that is structured into sections, with each section containing detailed and thought-provoking questions. The questions should test deep knowledge, including technical details, provenance considerations, and distinguishing features from similar antiques.

Responses should be written at a level appropriate for antiques experts.

Use Markup syntax for clarity, organizing the response into distinct sections.

variations.name: Master's Eye Training
variations.description: Upload a photo. You first receive a forensic checklist of what to examine. After you report your findings, you get an expert critique of your analysis and the correct conclusion. Choose this to train your eye and develop connoisseurship skills.
variations.template:
Role: You are a Polymath Expert in the decorative and fine arts, specializing in forensic analysis of antiques across all categories (furniture, ceramics, textiles, metalwork, etc.). Your goal is to train a serious collector to develop a "Master's Eye" through a two-step interactive process.

The Context: I will upload images of an antique (often from auction listings with varying quality). You must analyze these images with extreme rigor, looking for tool marks, material science, signs of aging, and evidence of restoration.

Step 1: The Forensic Checklist
Do NOT provide your full analysis yet. Instead, identify the object and provide a specific checklist of what I should examine in the images to determine age, quality, and authenticity. Focus heavily on forensic evidence (e.g., hand-planed vs. circular saw marks, irregular dovetails, honest wear vs. "distressing," glaze inclusions, or oxidation patterns).
Always include these three authentication tests in the checklist to surface possible modifications or inconsistencies:
• Structural Continuity: Have me trace the primary structural members (posts, stiles, rails, legs) looking for unexpected seams, shims, or grain misalignment at transition points. Any junction between components that should be continuous is a potential site of later addition or replacement.
• The Patina Test: Have me compare aging signatures (oxidation, grime accumulation, UV darkening, wear patterns) across all surfaces, especially between primary and secondary elements. Consistent patina gradients indicate a piece that has aged as a unit; any component that reads lighter, cleaner, or differently aged than its neighbors is a red flag for replacement or marriage.
• Stylistic Coherence: Have me evaluate whether every element—structural, decorative, and hardware—speaks the same period vocabulary. Inconsistencies such as ornate hardware on a plain case, decorative flourishes that contradict the form's original restraint, or mixed regional idioms suggest later "improvements" or assembled pieces.
Ask me to provide my observations on these specific points.

Step 2: The Master-Apprentice Critique
Once I provide my analysis, perform a "Master-Apprentice" critique.
• Direct Assessment: Rigorously evaluate my observations for accuracy and comprehensiveness. Correct me where I have missed a detail or misinterpreted a physical sign.
• Comparative Analysis: Contrast my findings with what an "ideal" or "textbook" authentic example of this piece should look like.
• The Reveal: Finally, provide your own expert conclusion on the item's likely period, origin, and condition based on the forensic evidence.

Instructions for Tone: Maintain a high-level, academic, yet practical tone. Use precise technical terminology (e.g., medullary rays, ormolu, craquelure, tenon and mortise). If the image quality is poor, tell me exactly what is obscured and what the "red flags" might be in that specific area.

Let's Begin: you can begin once the images are provided.

variations.name: Antiques Score
variations.description: Upload a photo. The piece is scored 0–10 on the Connoisseur's Decalogue — from modern reproduction to museum masterpiece — with specific evidence from the image supporting the rating. Choose this for quick quality assessment or comparison.
variations.template:
You are an antiques connoisseur trained to evaluate furniture and decorative objects using the Connoisseur's Decalogue—a rigorous 10-point quality scale.

When I upload a photo of an item, provide a numerical score (0-10) based on the following criteria:

**THE SCORING SCALE**

**The Baseline (0–2)**
• Score 0: Modern reproductions, CNC-machined pieces, or objects less than 100 years old
• Score 1: Genuine period piece with catastrophic loss (e.g., converted furniture, all legs replaced)
• Score 2: Standard 100+ year old object (late Victorian/Edwardian), mass-produced with industrial circular saws and uniform thicknessing

**The Industrial/Transition Gap (3–5)**
• Score 3: Mid-to-late 19th-century piece with early machine intervention, honest wear but lacks hand-work charm
• Score 4: Pre-1830 object that has been over-restored (stripped, dipped, patina killed)
• Score 5: Pre-industrial piece where two period elements have been joined ("marrying")

**The Pre-Industrial Threshold (6–10)**
Entry requires evidence of pre-1830 manufacture: hand-cut dovetails, pit-saw marks, hand-planed secondary woods

• Score 6: The Good Period Piece
  - Pre-industrial and aesthetically pleasing
  - Clear "hand-of-the-maker" evidence (irregular drawer bottoms, hand-planed surfaces)
  - May have replaced brasses or minor foot repairs
  - Finish likely a 20th-century refresh

• Score 7: The Distinguished Example
  - Superior design for its region (moves beyond "country" into "city" aesthetic)
  - 90%+ original components
  - Professional, non-invasive repairs only
  - Notable carving or joinery suggesting a master's hand

• Score 8: The Collector's Choice
  - Convergence of rarity of form and structural integrity
  - Old, deep finish (on piece for 100+ years, rich "alligator" texture)
  - Unusual variant (kettle-base desk, rare highboy)
  - No married parts; original secondary woods with expected oxidation

• Score 9: The Exceptional Survival
  - Purity of surface: "in the grease" untouched original 18th-century finish
  - Original hardware in original boring holes (no ghost marks)
  - Tool marks vibrant and crisp, not softened by over-polishing
  - Has spent life in darkened parlor or dry attic

• Score 10: The Museum Masterpiece
  - Perfect convergence of provenance, artistry, and condition
  - Form so rare only a handful known (e.g., signed Townsend/Goddard)
  - Pristine original surface exactly as it left the workshop
  - Clear documentation (bills of sale, single-family descent)
  - Superlative proportions and materials (flame mahogany, tiger maple)
  - "Time captured in amber"

**YOUR ASSESSMENT FORMAT**

Provide your evaluation using this structure:

**Score**: [0-10]

**Tier**: [Name of tier from the scale above]

**Evidence from Image**:
• [Manufacturing technique observations: hand vs. machine work, dovetails, saw marks]
• [Surface condition: patina, finish, oxidation patterns]
• [Structural elements: integrity, original components, repairs]
• [Hardware: original vs. replaced, boring holes, ghost marks]
• [Form and aesthetic: rarity, regional characteristics, proportions]
• [Tool marks and joinery visible in the photo]

**Rationale**:
[Explain how the visible evidence maps to the specific criteria for this score. What pushes the piece into this tier? What prevents it from scoring higher? What might elevate the score with closer inspection?]

**Assessment Limitations**:
[Be explicit about what cannot be determined from photographs alone. Note if multiple angles, close-ups of joinery, or hands-on inspection would be needed for a definitive assessment. Mention if image quality obscures critical details.]

**Key Principle**: "A '6' is a beautiful chair to sit in; a '10' is a sculpture that happens to be a chair."

Responses should be written at a level appropriate for serious collectors and antiques experts. Use precise technical terminology. Be rigorous and honest in your assessment.

variations.name: Conservation & Cleaning
variations.description: Upload a photo. You get a non-invasive conservation plan: finish identification, what's wrong, safe cleaning steps, how to stabilize loose joints, and which problems need a professional. Choose this before attempting any DIY care.
variations.template:
Role: Act as an expert Antique Conservator and Furniture Historian.
Task: Analyze the attached images of this object and provide a detailed assessment and a non-invasive conservation plan.
Goal: My objective is to clean, stabilize, and refresh this piece without destroying its original patina, historical integrity, or market value. I prioritize reversible, "conservation-grade" methods over permanent modern alterations.
Please provide the following:
 * Visual Assessment: Based on the photos, identify the likely era, wood species/materials, and the type of original finish (e.g., shellac, wax, oil, or varnish).
 * Condition Report: Identify visible issues such as "alligatoring," wood rot, failing veneer, loose joinery, or hardware oxidation.
 * Cleaning Protocol: Recommend the safest starting point for removing surface grime and old wax buildup without stripping the underlying finish.
 * Structural Stabilization: Suggest how to fix wobbles or loose joints using period-appropriate adhesives (like protein/hide glue) rather than modern PVA glues or mechanical fasteners.
 * Surface Refresh: Recommend specific conservation-grade products (e.g., Renaissance Wax, mineral spirits, or specific oils) to restore luster, and list common household or hardware store products to strictly avoid.
 * Professional Threshold: Identify which specific damage visible in these photos is too advanced for a DIYer and requires a professional conservator to prevent devaluation.
Constraint: Do not suggest aggressive sanding, chemical stripping to bare wood, or the use of modern polyurethane/silicone-based sprays. Focus on "minimal intervention."

title: Performance Review Assistant
description: Describe a project you worked on — it writes polished impact statements for your annual review, aligned to your firm's impact markers, with metrics and an enhanced version for each.
template:
Project Description: {{project_description}}
‍

Context: {{context}}

Annual Self-Assessment

For my annual performance review, I aim to measure my impact based on the provided set of impact markers. I require your assistance in synthesizing summaries for each of my projects, highlighting my contributions and aligning them with relevant impact markers.

Instructions:

Preliminary Research: Before diving into the Impact Statements, briefly study my background, professional style, and the provided impact markers to grasp the full context of my role, preferred methods of communication, and key performance indicators.
Project Deep Dive: Go beyond merely reading the project descriptions. Analyze them to understand the inherent challenges, strategic objectives, and the innovation they bring to the table.
Impact Statement Draft: Create an initial 'Impact Statement' for each project, synthesizing the project description with relevant impact markers. This statement should be a compelling narrative that highlights my role and contributions. Feel free to rearrange or rephrase elements for enhanced clarity and flow, while retaining the essence of each component.
Impact Metrics: For each Impact Statement, include specific metrics or quantifiable achievements that demonstrate the impact made. This could be in terms of cost savings, efficiency gains, or client engagement levels.
Enhanced Impact Statement: Generate an "Enhanced Impact Statement" that incorporates at least three specific refinements for clarity, impact, or strategic alignment. This version should not just be a reiteration but a polished narrative that builds upon the original Impact Statement, making it more persuasive and impactful.
Relevance Highlight: Conclude each Enhanced Impact Statement by explicitly indicating which impact markers were most crucial in shaping the project's success. This reinforces the alignment with organizational goals and objectives.
Narrative Flow: Ensure that both versions of the Impact Statement have a logical flow, transitioning smoothly from introducing the project, to discussing challenges, solutions, and finally, the impact created.
Background:

I'm a seasoned Product Designer at a prominent consulting firm, leading user experience design for our Global Banking Practice. As one of the four key figures in the central leadership team called the 'Global Banking Hub', my purview extends across the design and user experience of all banking solutions we offer. My approach hinges on the Human-Centered Design methodology for product design, ensuring that our tools are intuitive and effective for our end users. In product development, I adhere to Agile methodology, maintaining flexibility and responsiveness to deliver efficient, high-quality results. For problem solving and workshops, I rely on Design Thinking best practices..

My communication style prioritizes clarity and conciseness, without compromising on detail. I aim for substance and measurable impact, avoiding overconfidence and self-aggrandizement.

title: Webflow Code Helper
description: Describe what you want to build in Webflow — it asks clarifying questions, recommends an approach, then writes modular JavaScript with labeled sections you can safely swap without touching the rest.
template:
Objective: {{objective}}
‍

Context

I am using Webflow to build a prototype and would like your assistance achieving the 'Objective' above. I have limited experience editing JavaScript, so I need extremely modular, clearly labeled output to safely swap small sections without touching syntax.

Instructions

Part 1: Thorough Analysis of the Problem

Understanding the Challenge: Start by asking detailed questions to clearly understand the specific requirements and challenges of the task.
Identifying Requirements: pinpoint the requirements needed to achieve the coding challenge. This includes aspects like functionality, user experience, and technical specifications.
Exploring Solutions: Consider potential methods for addressing the challenge, weighing their advantages and disadvantages. Critically evaluate your proposed approach to identify any limitations. This step is crucial to ensure the method is viable and to address potential issues before moving on to code generation.
Recommendation: Based on your analysis, recommend the most suitable method for solving the challenge. You can say, "I evaluated multiple approaches and recommend...", but ONLY state the details of the optimal method to avoid verbose responses.
Part 2: Code

Code: Ensure all code snippets can be seamlessly integrated into my Webflow prototype.
Identifying Elements: Use custom data-* attributes for element identification (e.g., data-element="button", data-component="modal"). Specify the recommended attribute name and value(s) for each element the script needs.
Modular sections with markers: Break code into self-contained blocks surrounded by comment markers in this format:

// ---SECTION [FUNCTIONAL_NAME]---// [one-line description of this section's purpose][code for this section]// ---ENDSECTION [FUNCTIONAL_NAME]---

Use functional, hierarchical labels (e.g., FETCH_DATA, RENDER_BUTTON, VALIDATE_INPUT).
‍
Section map first: Before generating the full code, provide a brief outline of section names and their responsibilities. Wait for confirmation of that map before filling in the code.
Use a boilerplate template: Assume a one-page template already contains all comment markers. Only fill or replace code between existing markers so the structure remains intact.
Inline usage examples: At the bottom of each section, include a one-line snippet (e.g., initModal();) showing how it's invoked.
Incremental scope & isolated validation: Deliver each section in isolation. If possible, include a minimal test or example so I can validate one block at a time before merging.
Consistent formatting: Format all code (e.g., with Prettier) to ensure consistent indentation and braces. Do not alter comment markers.
Versioned backups: Whenever replacing a block, keep the previous version in a comment or separate file. This ensures easy rollback if something breaks.

Step 1:
Analyze the attached reference photographs to identify the subject's visual anchor: the fixed facial characteristics that must remain unchanged to preserve likeness. variations.template:
Step 1 — Likeness Lock + Visual Anchor extraction
"Analyze the attached likeness reference photos. Your primary objective is preserving identity.
Priority order: facial geometry and feature placement (highest) > expression > pose > lighting > wardrobe > background/painterly effects (lowest).
Define the subject's Visual Anchor as a set of non-negotiable constraints. Lock these elements: head/craniofacial silhouette, relative feature placement, eye spacing and tilt, eyelid shape, brow shape and distance to eyes, nose bridge/tip/nostril geometry, philtrum length, mouth width and lip shape, chin projection, jaw angle/width, and any distinctive asymmetries or markers.
Allowed changes: painterly rendering, skin finish, lighting design, color palette, edge softness, canvas/varnish effects.
Disallowed changes: altering facial proportions, moving features, beautifying asymmetries, changing age/ethnicity cues."

Step 2 — Output constraints, then request style reference
"Output the Visual Anchor as a bullet checklist of explicit constraints (10–20 items) written as 'must match' statements. Then provide a short compliance plan describing how you will keep the anchor intact during style transfer.
Then ask me for a Style Reference Image to proceed."

Step 3 — Generate
"Using the Style Reference Image, generate a 19th-century academic oil portrait that matches its brushwork, palette, lighting (chiaroscuro), composition conventions, and surface finish. The subject must remain immediately recognizable from the likeness references by strictly adhering to the Visual Anchor checklist.
Before finalizing, perform a quick internal cross-check: confirm each anchor item is satisfied."
Let's Begin: I am uploading the likeness references now.

Style: {{style}}
Clothing: {{clothing}}

If either field is blank, default to a refined classical academic oil portrait with simple, timeless clothing.

Analyze the uploaded reference images to identify and preserve the subject's Visual Anchor: the immutable facial characteristics that define their identity. This includes exact craniofacial structure, head shape, forehead height, cheekbone placement, jawline geometry, eye spacing and orientation, eyelid shape, nasal bridge and tip, mouth width and lip proportions, ear placement, and any distinctive asymmetries or contours. These features must remain anatomically unchanged to ensure immediate and unmistakable likeness.

Render the subject as a museum-quality oil portrait. If a custom style is provided, follow it while preserving likeness; otherwise, work in the tradition of refined academic painting.

Apply idealization only at the surface level—subtle refinement of skin texture, unified tonal transitions, and painterly handling of edges—while strictly preserving the subject's facial proportions, expression, and bone structure as seen in the reference images. Avoid transformation toward an archetypal face.

If custom clothing is provided, use it in a tasteful portrait-appropriate manner. Otherwise, clothing should be simple and classical in form, rendered in soft natural fabrics with minimal ornamentation, allowing focus to remain on the face. Brushwork should be smooth and intentional, conveying the materiality of oil paint without obscuring defining features.

The final image should read as a refined oil painting that depicts the same individual from the reference images, instantly recognizable, with identity fully preserved and expressed through a restrained, timeless painterly aesthetic.

variations.template: Turn this image into A 19th-century American architectural rendering in the style of Alexander Jackson Davis depicted as a precise front elevation orthographic drawing with no vanishing point. The artwork should utilize strict ruled ink lines overlaid with translucent watercolor washes in warm cream and pale yellow stone tones. Include a smooth graded wash sky fading from blue to white, deep calculated cast shadows to simulate depth within recesses, and high-contrast dark windows that maintain the window pattern of the reference. Any metal accents should be finished with verdigris green . Add a human scale figure at the entrance to establish scale, and faint greyed-out flanking structures to frame the subject.

Analyze the building shown in the uploaded image. Convert this structure into a vintage 19th-century architectural illustration plate, in the style of Alexander Jackson Davis.

The composition must be divided into two parts on a single sheet of aged, cream-colored parchment paper with visible foxing and texture:

- Top Half (Elevation): A precise, orthographic side-elevation drawing of the building from the image. Render it with fine ink linework and warm sepia watercolor washes for shading.
- Bottom Half (Plan): A plausible technical floor plan corresponding to the building above. Crucial Detail: All thick, cut wall sections within the floor plan must be filled entirely with a flat pink watercolor wash.

The overall aesthetic should look like a hand-drawn, academic architectural plate from the 1850s.

The figure stands upright with weight evenly distributed, one leg bearing slightly more load while the opposite leg relaxes, creating a subtle, restrained contrapposto.

The pelvis is level and stable; the torso is erect and open, with the rib cage lifted and aligned cleanly over the hips. The shoulders are relaxed and square, allowing the chest to read broadly and clearly.

Anatomy must be deliberate and canonical: clear articulation of rib cage, pelvis, scapulae, and major muscle groups, with emphasis on volume and proportion over surface texture.

Lighting is controlled and directional, atelier-style, using a single dominant light source to establish calm shadow masses and restrained highlights.

variations.template:
Act as a Master Fine Arts Instructor specializing in Classical Realism and Technical Art History. Analyze the uploaded reference image to provide a Stylistic Diagnosis—identifying if the work requires Classical Glazing, Alla Prima, Tonalism, or Impasto—and explain how this style dictates the physical handling of the paint. Provide a comprehensive, session-by-session guide designed for audio playback, written exclusively in dense, professional paragraphs without lists or tables.
Structure the audio guide into chronological chapters: The Foundation (Imprimatura and linear construction), The Underlayer (specific instructions for a Grisaille, Verdaccio, or Ebauche based on the diagnosis), The Development (building mid-tones and transitions), and The Finishing Touches (scumbling, glazing, and highlights). For each chapter, define the required 'Value' range and a specific limited palette (e.g., the Zorn Palette) with precise ratios for mixing skin tones.
Detail the chemical progression of mediums: define exactly when to use odorless mineral spirits versus refined linseed oil, and how to adjust the 'viscosity' and 'tack' of the paint to prevent lifting. Include instructions on 'Edge Control,' identifying where to maintain hard, soft, or 'lost' edges. Provide specific guidance on using calipers for anatomical verification against the printed reference, focusing on landmarks like the orbital sockets and the base of the columella.
Visual Benchmark Protocol: After each chapter description, explicitly remind me that I can ask for a 'Visual Stage Benchmark.' If I request a visualization of a specific stage, you must generate an image that accurately simulates how the original reference image would appear at that technical phase (e.g., a monochromatic version for a Grisaille, or a simplified, large-shape block-in for an Ebauche). These generated images must strictly adhere to the composition and lighting of the reference while reflecting the technical limitations of that specific stage of the painting process.
Maintain a rigorous, instructional tone, omitting all pleasantries and filler. At the very end of the response, include a brief closing statement (outside of the audio-format paragraphs) reminding me that I can ask for 'Detailed Instructions for Step X' to get specific brushwork techniques, or 'Visualize Stage X' to see the technical benchmark for my current progress.

variations.name: Watercolor Guide
variations.description: Upload a reference image. You get a phase-by-phase watercolor guide: paper choice, pigment transparency, wash sequence, and masking strategy — with instructions for generating visual examples of each in-progress stage.
variations.template:
Role: You are a Master Watercolorist and Art Instructor. Your goal is to help me reproduce the attached reference image using professional watercolor techniques.

Task 1: Stylistic & Technical Diagnosis
Analyze the attached image. Identify the watercolor approach best suited for this piece (e.g., Loose & Expressive, Botanical Realism, or Wet-on-Wet Impressionism).

Light Preservation: Identify the "pure whites" in the image and advise if I should use masking fluid or paint around them (negative painting).

Paper Choice: Suggest the ideal paper texture (Hot Press for detail, Cold Press for general use, or Rough for texture).

Task 2: Technical Breakdown

The Palette & Transparency: List the necessary pigments. Specify which should be Transparent, Semi-Opaque, or Granulating to match the textures in the reference.

Step-by-Step Guide: Break the process into logical watercolor phases:

Phase 1: The Sketch & Masking: (Guidelines for a light graphite sketch and preserving highlights).

Phase 2: The Initial Washes (Tea Consistency): (Establishing the lightest values and "under-glow" using wet-on-wet or flat washes).

Phase 3: Building Form (Milk Consistency): (Developing mid-tones, defining shapes, and managing edges—soft vs. hard).

Phase 4: Values & Shadows (Honey Consistency): (Adding the darkest "darks," dry-brush textures, and fine details).

Task 3: Interactive Guidance
Provide high-level instructions for these steps now. End your response by reminding me that I can ask for:

"Detailed Instructions for Step X" (including specific water-to-paint ratios and brush types).

"Generate a visual reference for Step X" (to see a generated example of the painting in its "ugly duckling" mid-wash stage).

variations.name: Drawing Guide
variations.description: Upload a reference image. You get a phase-by-phase drawing guide: pencil grades, value mapping, blending decisions, and eraser-highlight techniques — with instructions for generating visual examples of each stage.
variations.template:
Role: You are a Master Draftsman and Drawing Instructor. Your goal is to help me reproduce the attached reference image as a high-quality pencil drawing.

Task 1: Stylistic & Technical Diagnosis
Analyze the attached image. Identify the drawing approach best suited for this piece (e.g., Photorealism, Expressive Sketching, Classical Academic Study, or High-Contrast Tonalism).

Material Recommendations: Suggest the best medium for the job (e.g., Standard Graphite, Charcoal for deep blacks, or Carbon pencil for matte finishes) and necessary tools (kneaded erasers, blending stumps, or specific paper "tooth").

Texture Strategy: Briefly explain the primary technique needed for the dominant textures in the image (e.g., "Use tight circulisim for the skin pores," or "Use aggressive cross-hatching for the fabric folds").

Task 2: Technical Breakdown
Based on your diagnosis, provide:

The "Palette" (Grade Selection): List the specific range of pencils required (e.g., "You will need a hard HB for layout, a versatile 2B for mid-tones, and a soft 8B for darkest shadows").

Step-by-Step Guide: Divide the process into logical drawing phases that build value gradually:

Phase 1: The Framework (Hard Pencils): (Guidelines for accurate proportions, gesture, and light "envelope" shapes).

Phase 2: Mapping Shadows (Mid-Range Pencils): (Establishing the "bedbug line" separating light and shadow families; flat tonal mapping).

Phase 3: Modeling Form & Blending: (Developing gradients, turning soft edges, and deciding whether to blend with tools or rely on distinct hatch marks).

Phase 4: Deepening Darks & Texture: (Pushing contrast to the limit with softest pencils and rendering specific surface details).

Phase 5: Subtractive Highlights: (Using erasers to "draw" back into the tones to create crisp highlights or stray hairs).

Task 3: Interactive Guidance
Provide high-level instructions for these steps now. End your response by reminding me that I can ask for:

"Detailed Instructions for Step X" (including specific pencil grades, pressure guidance, and stroke types).

"Generate a visual reference for Step X" (to see a generated example of what the drawing should look like at that specific stage of completion, e.g., just the line work).

variations.name: Blocking-In Study
variations.description: Upload a finished oil painting — it generates a new image showing what the initial blocking-in stage would look like: broad flat color masses with no detail. Choose Low fidelity for a pure abstract color map, Mid for a version with secondary planes and color temperature.

variables.name: fidelity
variables.label: Fidelity
optionDescriptions: Pure abstract color map with flat shapes, no edge variation or temperature shifts
Adds secondary planes, color temperature variation, and edge differentiation

variations.template:
Analyze the attached finished oil painting and generate a new image that visualizes its blocking-in stage. Fidelity: {{fidelity}}.

Fidelity level definitions — apply only the one selected above:

* Low — Reduce all forms to the broadest flat primary masses. Use a single flat local color per region. No secondary planes, edge variation, directional brushwork, or temperature shifts. The result should read as a pure abstract color map.

* Mid — Reduce forms to primary masses but allow secondary planes where major surfaces turn. Introduce color temperature variation within masses (warm lights, cool shadows). Differentiate hard edges at form boundaries from soft edges at gradual transitions. Suggest volume with directional brushwork. Still no fine textures, highlights, or rendered detail.

Constraints common to all fidelity levels:

* Composition: Maintain the exact proportions and layout of the original.
* Style: Thin underpainting aesthetic — a 'lean' first layer or lay-in. A front-on map of the painting's masses.
* Presentation: Flat, front-on perspective. No framing, easel, or context — just the study itself.

The goal is to strip away the 'rendered' data (skin texture, leaf detail) and reveal only the 'abstract' data beneath it.

variations.name: Archival Studio Catalog
variations.description: Upload a photo of any object. The model corrects perspective, replaces the background with a clean studio neutral, and balances lighting — while preserving every scratch, chip, and patina detail. Choose this to create archival-quality catalog photos of antiques or collectibles.
variations.template:
Role: Act as a professional archival photographer and digital conservator.

Task: Transform the attached reference image into a high-end, studio-quality photograph.

Technical Corrections:

Perspective & Geometry: Correct all lens distortion and keystoning. Ensure all architectural lines are perfectly vertical and horizontal. Align the camera angle to be perfectly square (orthographic) to the subject.

Lighting: Replace uneven lighting, harsh shadows, or phone-flash glare with balanced, diffused studio "clamshell" lighting. Ensure even illumination across the entire frame with neutral white balance.

Optics: Remove digital noise and chromatic aberration. Ensure edge-to-edge sharpness as if shot with a prime macro lens at $f/8$ on a full-frame sensor.

Subject Integrity (Crucial):

Preservation: Do NOT "beautify" or "repair" the subject. Maintain every nuance of the original texture, including scratches, chips, fabric pills, patina, or weathered edges. The subject must remain an exact 1:1 replica of the input's physical state.

Materiality: Enhance the tactile quality of the materials (e.g., the weave of the fabric, the glaze of the ceramic, the grain of the paper) without altering their form.

Final Output: A clean, professional product catalog shot against a neutral, non-distracting studio background. High dynamic range, 8k resolution, photorealistic.

Optional user notes (may be blank; if blank, ignore this field): {{additional_notes}}

variations.name: Luxury Magazine Refinement
variations.description: Upload any photograph. The model refines it into a high-fidelity, magazine-quality image — tilt-shift perspective correction, soft natural lighting with HDR roll-off, and tactile material detail. Choose this to elevate an interior or architectural photo to the standard of a luxury publication while preserving the original scene.
variations.template:
Transform this uploaded reference image into a high-fidelity, magazine-quality photograph suitable for a luxury publication.

Critical Execution: Upscale and reconstruct. Treat the input as a spatial reference only. If the input is low-resolution, degraded, or blurry, synthesize new, sharp, high-definition textures to replace pixelated areas. Restore clarity to fine details, ensuring every element has the crispness consistent with an 8K full-frame DSLR capture.

Technical Requirements: Emulate a 24mm tilt-shift lens capture for perfect vertical alignment and sophisticated perspective without distortion.

Lighting & Depth: Implement a natural lighting scheme using soft, diffused indirect sunlight. Prioritize 'Global Illumination' to create authentic micro-shadows in corners and crevices. The image must feature high dynamic range (HDR) with realistic highlight roll-off and deep shadow detail.

Materiality: Uphold the materiality of the original scene. Ensure tactile quality: wood grain must show subtle pores, fabrics visible weaves, and stone natural matte or polished reflections.

Aesthetic Filter: Avoid any 'plastic,' hyper-smooth, or overly-processed AI artifacts. Maintain a slight, natural film grain. The final output must be indistinguishable from a physical photograph.

Optional user notes (may be blank; if blank, ignore this field): {{additional_notes}}

variations.name: Tina Barney Style Photo
variations.description: Upload any photo. The model reinterprets it with Tina Barney's large-format aesthetic: naturalistic color, painterly palette, dimensional skin tones, and quiet psychological presence. Choose this for portraiture and interior scenes.
variations.template:
Convert this photograph while preserving the original characters, setting, composition, and narrative context exactly as captured. Reinterpret the image with fine-art, editorial quality inspired by Tina Barney's photographic style.

Apply the visual language of large-format, museum-grade color photography: naturalistic yet deliberate color, soft but controlled contrast, and a restrained, painterly palette. Skin tones should appear realistic and dimensional, with subtle warmth and tonal depth. Whites should be creamy rather than stark; shadows should retain detail without appearing flat or crushed.

Maintain a sense of quiet psychological presence and observational intimacy. The image should feel composed, intentional, and slightly detached—neither cinematic nor stylized for drama. Avoid exaggerated sharpness, HDR effects, or artificial lighting artifacts.

Upgrade the technical quality to resemble a contemporary high-end digital or medium-format camera: refined dynamic range, clean micro-detail, smooth tonal transitions, and film-like color depth without visible grain. Preserve natural imperfections and avoid cosmetic retouching that would flatten character or environment.

The final result should read as gallery-ready fine art photography—editorial in rigor, restrained in execution, and suitable for museum or print publication.

variations.name: Sally Mann Style Photo
variations.description: Upload any photo. The model renders it as a large-format wet-plate collodion photograph: gloomy, high-contrast, with swirling bokeh and subtle chemical imperfections. Choose this for a haunting, antique photographic quality.
variations.template: Keep the current subject but render the image as a large format black and white photograph in the style of Sally Mann's wet-plate collodion process. adjust the zoom if needed to achieve proper balance. The aesthetic should feel gloomy and feature the unique optical qualities of antique lenses, including a very shallow depth of field with sharp selective focus on the main subject, rapidly falling off into a painterly, swirling bokeh and soft atmospheric blur. The lighting is high-contrast but refined for a fine art print, with rich, deep blacks retaining subtle detail, and luminous, glowing whites. Instead of heavy damage, incorporate subtle, organic imperfections inherent to the hand-made process: delicate chemical swirls, faint emulsion irregularities near the edges, and soft, natural vignetting. The final texture should feel tactile, timeless, and haunting, possessing the quality of a masterfully crafted, hand-printed photograph suitable for framing.

variations.template: Transform this antique photograph into a seamless, high-quality color photograph. The colorization should be completely natural, replicating the aesthetic of a photo taken with a contemporary color camera, while preserving the original subject and setting without any changes.

variations.name: Photographic Rendering
variations.description: Convert a provided image mockup into a photorealistic rendering
variations.template:
Convert the provided composite mockup into a photorealistic architectural rendering suitable for a high-end design portfolio.

Core Directives:

Structural Fidelity: This design represents my intentions, I just want you to refine it. Do not add or subtract elements unless requested. Ensure existing elements are perfectly integrated as any part of the provided image may have been pasted in from elsewhere and may not be consistent with perspective, lighting, scale, etc. 

Perspective & Geometry: Automatically correct any lens distortion, "keystoning," or skewed angles. Ensure all vertical lines are perfectly parallel to the frame edges (Standard Architectural 2-Point Perspective).

Materiality & Texture: Uphold the texture and materiality but increase fidelity so the image is consistent with a professional quality DSLR. 

Lighting & Atmosphere: Implement a sophisticated natural lighting scheme. 

Photographic Quality: Emulate a full-frame DSLR capture using a wide-angle tilt-shift lens. The final output must be indistinguishable from a physical photograph, featuring natural micro-shadows and high dynamic range (HDR) without looking "plastic" or over-processed.

title: Component Adaptation (Design System)
description: Name a UI component and provide a screenshot — it runs a full adaptation workflow: color extraction, token mapping, Web Component build, state testing, and pixel-perfect verification at ≥99.9% match.
template:
Component: {{component_name}}

I need to adapt this component to my m3-design-v2 design system with PERFECT visual fidelity. I will provide a screenshot and original code. Follow the enhanced systematic workflow with automated color extraction, state testing, and pixel-perfect verification. CRITICAL: Perfect visual fidelity is non-negotiable - no close enough approximations allowed.

Phase 1 Color Extraction and Analysis. First extract ALL colors from the reference screenshot using an automated Playwright script. The script should navigate to the reference, query all elements, and extract computed color values for every text element, background, border, and interactive state. Create a comprehensive color mapping table showing reference color in RGB, hex value, closest design system token, whether it's an exact match, and what action is required. If a design system semantic token like var(--md-sys-color-on-surface) does NOT match the reference color exactly check the delta E, if delta E is 2.0 or higher you MUST create component-specific color tokens. Never approximate or use close enough colors. For example if the reference uses Tailwind stone-900 rgb(28,25,23) but design system on-surface is rgb(18,23,20), the delta E is high, so create --wy-component-title-color with the exact reference value. Document why semantic tokens don't work for this specific design. This prevents the critical error of using wrong semantic tokens that cause subtle visual hierarchy mismatches.

Phase 1 continued Analysis and Planning. Analyze the screenshot for layout structure, typography hierarchy, interactive elements, spacing patterns, and visual details like border radius shadows and transitions. Review the original code for hardcoded values to replace. Look for color hex codes, pixel spacing, font sizes and families, border radius values, and transition durations. Create a complete token mapping table. Identify missing design system assets.

Phase 2 Design System Updates. If analysis reveals colors that don't match existing tokens exactly, add new component-specific color tokens to m3-design-v2/src/styles/tokens.css. Follow naming pattern --wy-component-name-property-color. Always include dark mode variants in @media prefers-color-scheme dark block. Add spacing shape or motion tokens if required. Document rationale for each new token explaining why semantic tokens don't provide exact match. Use existing patterns, semantic names, provide dark mode variants, and document rationale in comments.

Phase 3 Implementation. Create Web Component in m3-design-v2/src/components/wy-[name].js using LitElement. Define reactive properties. Import fonts in Shadow DOM for Playfair Display and Material Symbols Outlined. Use design system tokens exclusively with absolutely zero hardcoded values. Define component-specific color tokens in :host block with fallback values like --wy-component-title-color: #1C1917. Use these specific tokens in styles not generic semantic tokens. Follow Material Design 3 state layer patterns using ::before pseudo-elements with opacity transitions. Add focus-visible outlines 3px solid with 2px offset. Never use !important. Use semantic HTML. Component structure: import from lit, extend LitElement, declare static properties, define static styles with all design system tokens, define component-specific color tokens in :host, implement state layers for interactive elements, implement render method.

Phase 4 Build and Prepare Testing. Build design system with npm run build, start dev server with npm run dev, create test page if needed importing tokens and component.

Phase 5.1 Color Accuracy Testing MANDATORY. Create and run test-color-accuracy.py script. Extract reference colors from original using Playwright script that loads reference and extracts computed getComputedStyle values for all text backgrounds and borders converting to RGB tuples. Define REFERENCE_COLORS dictionary with element name to RGB tuple mapping like title: (28, 25, 23) for stone-900. Extract implementation colors using same method from component shadow root. Calculate color delta using Euclidean distance formula. For each color verify delta E is less than 2.0 which means imperceptible difference. Delta E between 2.0 and 5.0 is noticeable, above 5.0 is clearly visible. Print formatted table showing element, actual RGB, expected RGB, and delta value. Success criteria: ALL colors must have delta E less than 2.0. If test fails identify which colors have high delta, verify component uses correct component-specific tokens not generic semantic tokens, ensure tokens defined in :host block for Shadow DOM, re-run extraction to verify reference colors, update token values. Common failures: using var(--md-sys-color-on-surface) when reference uses Tailwind stone palette, tokens not cascading into Shadow DOM, circular variable references like --spacing-sm: var(--spacing-sm, ...), CDN cache serving old bundle.

Phase 5.2 Interactive State Testing MANDATORY. Extract state colors from reference for hover focus active and disabled states using Playwright to hover elements, press Tab for focus, click for active. For each interactive element verify default rest state colors match reference exactly with delta E less than 2.0, hover state border background color changes match, focus state keyboard indicator matches with outline style color width and offset, active pressed state click appearance matches, disabled state opacity cursor and colors match if applicable, loading state indicators match if applicable, error state styling matches if applicable, and state transitions animation duration and easing match reference. Test all interactive states for buttons chips links form inputs. Success criteria: all state colors match reference with delta E less than 2.0, state transitions occur, no states missing, focus indicators meet WCAG. Common issues: using :hover without state layer pseudo-element, missing :focus-visible styles, hardcoding state colors instead of state layer opacity, missing disabled state, transitions too fast or slow.

Phase 5.3 Pixel Perfect Visual Comparison MANDATORY. Use Playwright plus PIL to capture screenshots from reference and implementation at exact same viewport like 1200x900. Convert images to RGB numpy arrays. Calculate per-pixel difference using absolute value of array subtraction. Create diff heatmap where pixels within tolerance of 2 RGB points per channel are green and pixels outside tolerance are red. Calculate match percentage as matching pixels divided by total pixels times 100. Generate visual outputs: diff heatmap PNG with green for matching red for different, side-by-side comparison PNG with reference on left implementation on right with labels. Success criteria: match percentage must be 99.9% or higher allowing only 0.1% for anti-aliasing differences, no visible differences in side-by-side, diff heatmap shows only minor anti-aliasing if any. Acceptable differences: font rendering 1-2px due to browser OS, anti-aliasing on rounded corners within tolerance, sub-pixel rendering under 2 points per channel. Unacceptable: wrong colors different hue saturation lightness, wrong spacing margin padding gap offsets, wrong font sizes or weights, wrong border radius or shapes, missing elements or incorrect positioning. If match under 99.9%: review heatmap to identify problem areas, check if acceptable anti-aliasing or real issues, increase tolerance slightly if only anti-aliasing max 5, if real differences fix implementation and retest, never lower success threshold.

Phase 5.4 Functional Testing. Verify all original functionality preserved, test modal open close, click handlers, navigation, keyboard shortcuts, event dispatching, state management. Run automated functional test or manual verification.

Phase 5.5 Design System Compliance. Scan component code for hardcoded hex codes, magic number spacing, arbitrary durations, !important declarations. Verify 100% token usage. Zero hardcoded values allowed.

Phase 6 Deliverables. Provide: 1 Color extraction report table showing reference RGB, hex value, design system token, whether exact match, and action taken. 2 Token mapping report complete table original value to token with status. 3 Color accuracy test results showing all delta E values under 2.0 with formatted table and max delta reported. 4 Interactive state test results showing all states verified with hover focus active disabled tested and screenshot evidence. 5 Pixel perfect comparison results with match percentage 99.9% or higher, diff heatmap image path, side-by-side comparison image path, and acceptable vs unacceptable differences documented. 6 Complete component code with full Web Component implementation, registration in src/main.js, component.json entry with props and examples, usage example in markup. 7 Design system updates if any showing new tokens added to tokens.css, dark mode variants, and rationale for why semantic tokens didn't work. 8 Integration instructions for using component, available API, event listeners, build and deploy commands.

Success criteria ALL must pass: Color accuracy with all elements delta E under 2.0 imperceptible difference, pixel match with 99.9% or higher match percentage, all interactive states tested and verified matching reference, visual fidelity where component matches screenshot exactly, token usage 100% design tokens zero hardcoded values, Playwright tests all passing, dark mode works in both schemes, no console errors, semantic HTML, ARIA labels present, focus states visible and accessible.

Anti-patterns to avoid: hardcoded colors hex rgb rgba, magic number spacing, arbitrary transitions, !important declarations, changing background-color on hover directly, hardcoded border-radius, skipping dark mode testing, forgetting Shadow DOM fonts, inline styles for theming, delivering without verification, using generic semantic tokens when reference needs specific colors, assuming semantic token names match without verifying RGB values, testing functionality before colors are correct, approximating colors instead of exact match.

Design system compliance for colors: use var(--md-sys-color-*) or var(--wy-*), provide dark mode variants, use color-mix() for transparency. Typography: use var(--font-serif) var(--font-sans) var(--font-display), clamp() for fluid scaling, design system letter spacing. Spacing: use var(--spacing-*) xxs through 3xl, follow 8px grid, clamp() for responsive. Shape: use var(--md-sys-shape-corner-*), corner-full for capsules. Motion: use var(--md-sys-motion-duration-*) and var(--md-sys-motion-easing-*), no magic durations. Interactive states: hover with ::before overlay, opacity var(--md-sys-state-hover-opacity), focus 3px outline 2px offset, never change background directly.

Execute complete workflow with all testing phases 5.1 through 5.5 and provide all 8 deliverables with color extraction report, color accuracy delta E results, state testing results, pixel perfect comparison with heatmap and match percentage, complete code, design system updates, and integration instructions.

title: Essay Topic Discovery
description: Enter a broad topic — it decomposes it into conceptual pillars, generates essay ideas from each, optionally filters by your interests, and produces a ready-to-use audio essay script for the topic you choose.

steps.name: Conceptual Decomposition
steps.instructions: Start by entering your topic. This step will identify conceptual pillars that define your topic.
steps.template:
Context: This is step 1 of a multi-step prompt, intended to be completed in phases.

Topic: {{topic}}

Role: You are a conceptual analysis and brainstorming engine. Your task is to decompose this topic into its core conceptual pillars—the major dimensions that collectively define, explain, or frame the topic.

Task
Analyze the topic and identify a set of distinct, high-level conceptual pillars that meaningfully structure understanding of the topic.

A conceptual pillar is:

A major lens, dimension, or category of thought

Mutually distinguishable from other pillars

Broad enough to support deeper ideation later

Relevant regardless of the user’s background

Examples of pillar types (use only when relevant):

Technical / Mechanistic

Historical / Evolutionary

Structural / Systemic

Economic / Incentive-based

Social / Cultural

Psychological / Cognitive

Ethical / Normative

Operational / Practical

Strategic / Competitive

Legal / Regulatory

Instructions

Do not explain the topic itself.

Do not ideate within the pillars yet.

Focus on coverage and clarity, not exhaustiveness.

Prefer 5–9 pillars unless the topic strongly suggests otherwise.

Each pillar should be clearly differentiated and non-overlapping.

Output Format

Present the results as a numbered list.

For each pillar:

Provide a concise title (2–5 words).

Include a one-sentence description explaining what aspect of the topic this pillar captures.

Output Example (format only)

Technical Foundations – The core mechanisms, tools, or technologies that enable the topic.

Historical Context – How the topic emerged and evolved over time.

Economic Dynamics – Incentives, costs, and value creation surrounding the topic.

After listing the primary conceptual pillars, identify 3–5 cross-cutting axes, tensions, or continua that cut across multiple pillars.

Each axis should:

Represent a meaningful spectrum (not a category)

Apply to more than one pillar

Suggest potential conflict, trade-off, or synthesis

Examples (generic)

Centralization ↔ Decentralization

Human judgment ↔ Automation

Stability ↔ Adaptability

Short-term efficiency ↔ Long-term resilience

Output Format

Axis: <Name> — <One-sentence explanation>

Finally, optionally identify up to two emergent or blended lenses that arise from the interaction of multiple pillars. These should not replace primary pillars and should only be included if they reveal a qualitatively different way of thinking about the topic.
---

After presenting the results, instruct the user to share instructions for Step 2.

variables.name: topic
variables.label: Topic
variables.placeholder: e.g., Greek Revival Architecture, Bourbon Production, Salem Maritime Trade

steps.name: Topic Ideation
steps.instructions: Expands on the pillars from Step 1 to generate related topics.
steps.template:
{{pillars}}

Step 2: This step builds on the pillars provided above or established in the previous step. 

This step has two distinct parts:

Pillar-Anchored Essay Topics – Generate essay ideas derived from each conceptual pillar.

Free Essay Topics – Generate additional essay ideas that are not anchored to any pillar or formal rule, other than being intellectually interesting, original, and non-obvious.

Part A: Pillar-Anchored Essay Topics

For each conceptual pillar, generate essay topics that explore the topic through that specific lens.

Each pillar-anchored essay topic should:

Clearly draw inspiration from the pillar’s perspective

Suggest enough complexity, breadth, or narrative richness to sustain a long-form essay

Instructions

Process the pillars one at a time.

Generate 2–3 essay topics per pillar.

Ensure topics across different pillars feel meaningfully distinct.

Avoid generic or encyclopedic framings (e.g., “An overview of…”).

Output Format (Part A)

Pillar:

Essay Topic 1: <Concise, interesting title or framing>

Essay Topic 2: <Concise, interesting title or framing>

Essay Topic 3 (optional): <Concise, interesting title or framing>

Part B: Free Essay Topics (Unanchored)

After completing the pillar-based ideation, generate 10 additional essay topics that are not explicitly tied to any single pillar.

These topics should:

Be optimized for interest to a highly intelligent listener or reader

Feel surprising, counterintuitive, or curiosity-inducing even to a highly intelligent audience with background knowledge of the subject

Potentially cut across multiple pillars—or ignore them entirely

Be driven by intriguing framings, obscure information, curious historical connections, or general interdisciplinary connectedness that makes this feel like a web if interest.

Think of these as the ideas that emerge after structure has done its work—where creative synthesis, intuition, and originality take over.

Constraints

Do not reference pillars or the decomposition process in these ideas.

Do not reuse or lightly remix pillar-anchored topics.

Free Essay Topics Tone & Quality Bar

Intellectually ambitious

Curious, sharp, and exploratory

Optimized for idea generation rather than polish

Success Criteria
A successful output produces a diverse set of essay topics where:

The pillar-based topics reflect disciplined breadth

The free topics demonstrate creative synthesis and originality

Any single topic could plausibly sustain a deep, engaging 3,000-word essay without filler

Important: All suggested topics should have a unique number so the user can easily identify a specific topic in the next step.

variables.name: pillars
variables.label: Pillars (from Step 1)
variables.placeholder: Only needed if starting a new chat

steps.name: Optional: Interest Alignment
steps.instructions: Optional step: Align topic suggestions to personal interests.

Credit and merchant finance (notes, bills of exchange, collateral, insolvency, reputational capital)
Insurance and risk pricing (marine, fire; loss documentation, fraud, actuarial thinking in everyday life)
Law and property regimes (admiralty, probate, trusts, incorporation; who owns what, and when it can be seized)
Logistics and governance (customs houses, quarantine, lighthouses, harbor masters, inspection regimes)
Information systems (newspapers, circulars, ledgers, catalogues, society columns; how reputations travel)

These interests should influence what feels worth exploring, not what is permitted.

Inputs

The topic list generated in Step 2 (pillar‑anchored topics and free topics)

The personal interest signals listed above

Role & Objective

You are an intellectual curator and idea‑generation engine.

Your task is not to write the essay yet. Your task is to generate and refine essay topic ideas that:

Are aligned with the stated personal interests

Would plausibly sustain a two‑thousand‑five‑hundred‑word audio essay

Remain original, non‑obvious, and intellectually demanding

This step exists to translate raw ideation into high‑conviction essay candidates.

Use of Personal Interest Signals

Personal interest signals should be treated as:

A way to guide selection, emphasis, and recombination

A signal of intellectual taste, not a filter

They may be used to:

Elevate topics that resonate strongly with the interests

Reframe or sharpen topics to better align with those interests

Inspire new topics that were not explicitly present in Step 2

They must not be used to:

Narrow the conceptual scope too aggressively

Eliminate strong ideas solely due to weak interest alignment

Produce only one type of argument or lens

At least some topics should stretch beyond the user’s stated interests while remaining compelling.

Instructions

Review all essay topics generated in Step 2.

Select and refine five to seven essay topics that best balance:

Alignment with personal interests

Intellectual depth and originality

Capacity to sustain long‑form, audio‑friendly treatment

Generate three to five additional new essay topics inspired by the interests but not directly lifted from Step 2.

Ensure diversity of perspective, mechanism, and argumentative posture across the final set.

Output Format
Refined Essay Topics (from Step 2)

For each topic:

Essay Topic:

Why It’s Compelling: One to two sentences explaining the intellectual hook and depth

New Interest‑Inspired Essay Topics

For each topic:

Essay Topic:

Why It’s Compelling: One to two sentences explaining the intellectual hook and depth

Tone & Quality Bar

Discerning, curious, and intellectually ambitious

Focused on what is worth thinking about, not how to explain basics

Optimized for helping a sophisticated reader decide what they want to explore next

Success Criteria

A successful output produces a short list of essay topics that:

Feel personally resonant without becoming narrow

Invite deep exploration rather than summary

Would clearly justify a long‑form audio essay

variables.name: interests
variables.label: Personal Interests
variables.placeholder: (e.g., Greek Revival Architecture, Battery Technology, French Academic Drawing...)

steps.name: Audio Essay Generation
steps.instructions: Select an essay topic, then paste this prompt into a new chat to generate the audio essay script.
steps.template:
Topic: {{selected_abstract}}

Write a 2500 word essay on the topic above.

The essay should read as a continuous, intellectually rigorous narrative, suitable for listening as an audio essay or as a chapter in a serious nonfiction book read aloud by a professional narrator.

Structure the essay entirely in paragraphs.
Do not use lists, bullet points, outlines, headings, or section labels.

Assume the reader is highly intelligent, well-read, and impatient with filler.

Narrative & Structural Requirements

Maintain a single, coherent argumentative throughline from opening paragraph to conclusion.

Each paragraph must advance the argument, introduce new information, or deepen a previously established claim.

Avoid summaries, signposting, or meta-commentary about the structure of the essay.

Transitions should be implicit and logical, not announced.

The essay should feel dense but readable, with ideas accumulating rather than repeating.

Style and Language Rules
Numbers, Dates, and Symbols

Spell out all numbers and dates fully.

Do not use numerals, symbols, or shorthand.

Do not use abbreviations or acronyms unless spelled out in full on first use.

Do not use symbols such as percent signs, currency symbols, or ampersands; write the words instead.

Citations and References

Do not use inline citations, parentheticals, footnotes, or bracketed references.

All sources must be collected at the end in a concise References section.

The body of the essay should integrate facts naturally, without citation markers.

Sentence Discipline

Write in complete sentences.

Favor precise verbs and concrete nouns.

Avoid rhetorical questions.

Linguistic Constraints (Strict)

The following are disallowed:

Throat-clearing phrases
Examples: “It is worth noting,” “Let us begin,” “As we will see,” “This essay explores.”

Decorative or atmospheric adjectives
Examples: stately, haunting, evocative, rich, vibrant, sweeping.

Litotes or indirect claims
Avoid constructions like “not insignificant,” “not uncommon,” or “not unlike.”
Use direct assertions.

Personification of abstractions
History does not “speak,” “whisper,” or “remember.”
Institutions do not “want” or “feel.”
Use causal, structural, or material explanations instead.

Narrative scaffolding or curator voice
No previews, summaries, or commentary on what the essay is doing.

Audience & Intellectual Depth

Write for an audience that:

Already knows the basic facts

Expects originality, synthesis, and precision

Is interested in second-order effects, hidden constraints, and structural consequences

To meet this bar:

Integrate historical, technical, cultural, or economic context where relevant.

Anchor claims with specific details: dates, figures, mechanisms, names, or documented events.

Acknowledge credible counterarguments or alternative interpretations when they matter, and explain why one perspective ultimately proved more influential or accurate.

Use analogies or metaphors only when they increase clarity, not atmosphere.

Factual Rigor

Do not generalize without support.

Avoid vague phrases such as “many experts believe” or “it is widely understood.”

When uncertainty exists, specify its source and scope.

Maintain internal consistency of facts, timelines, and causal claims.

If precise data is unavailable, say so explicitly and explain why.

Tone

Serious, analytical, and exacting

Calm and confident, never performative

Clear without simplification

Aim for the cadence of a long-form essay in a serious intellectual magazine or a chapter in a rigorous nonfiction book.

Conclusion and Extensions

At the end of the essay:

Include a References section listing the sources used.

After the references, ask the user whether they would like recommendations for further exploration.
These recommendations should assume a sophisticated reader and point toward:

Influential books

Primary sources

Advanced essays

Serious lectures or archival materials

Do not include these recommendations unless the user explicitly asks for them in a follow-up.

Context Handling Instruction

If this prompt appears in a new conversation, treat the topic as fully self-contained.
If this prompt appears in a continuing conversation, do not reference earlier steps, brainstorming, or selection logic. Write the essay as a finished standalone work.

title: Fabric Colorways
description: Upload a fabric design — it recolors it using a palette image you provide, or generates four colorway variations based on a style direction.
